name: Sundara-Pandya
dates_notes: Pre-Śaṅkara; cited by Sureśvara in the Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi; verses preserved as quotations only.
core_thesis: As reconstructed from later citations: Sundara-Pāṇḍya is a pre-Śaṅkara author known almost entirely through verses preserved as citations in Sureśvara's *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* (and a handful of other Advaita prakaraṇas). The corpus that survives — perhaps a dozen verses — is read by Sureśvara as a friendly, almost-Advaita authority: the verses defend the immediacy of *ātma-jñāna* as the direct cause of *mokṣa* against *jñāna-karma-samuccaya*, treat the *jīva* as not-other than Brahman in its essential nature, and articulate the inadequacy of *karma* and ritual to produce the liberating apprehension. He is therefore standardly classed by the tradition as a proto-Advaitin: not a fully developed Śaṅkara-style *māyāvādin*, but a Vedāntin whose verses already hold the structural commitments — non-difference, the directness of *jñāna*, the inertness of *karma* with respect to *mokṣa* — that Śaṅkara's school will systematize. Nothing of him beyond the cited verses is recoverable: not his school-affiliation, not his literary corpus, not his geographical or chronological context.

engaged_works.title: Lost verses cited in Naishkarmya-Siddhi
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Verse-corpus is lost; preserved only as citations within Sureśvara's Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi.
engaged_works.summary: Sundara-Pāṇḍya's literary corpus is lost. What survives is a set of verses (the most-cited cluster appears in *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* book 1, where Sureśvara invokes them as authoritative against the *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* opponent) which Sureśvara introduces with explicit attribution and treats as authoritative on the side of immediate *jñāna* as the direct cause of *mokṣa*. The verses articulate (i) the essential non-difference of the self and Brahman as the upaniṣadic teaching's settled meaning, (ii) the structural unfitness of *karma* — bound to action, agency, and *kartṛtva*-falsity — to be a means to a liberation that consists in the cessation of all such superimpositions, and (iii) the sufficiency of correct upaniṣadic apprehension. This is the basis for the standard tradition-internal classification of Sundara-Pāṇḍya as a proto-Advaitin or near-Advaitin pre-Śaṅkara authority. Nothing of his original work, its title, or its full extent can be recovered.
